items.q: How do vaccines protect you from a disease?
items.correct: They expose your immune system to a harmless form of the pathogen so memory cells form, allowing rapid response if exposed later.
items.misconception: Vaccines kill the pathogen if it ever enters the body.
items.random_wrong: Vaccines provide antibodies for life by adding them directly to the blood.
items.why: Students think vaccines actively fight pathogens themselves, rather than priming the immune system to fight.

items.q: Why is the secondary immune response to a pathogen faster and stronger than the primary?
items.correct: Memory cells generated during the primary response can rapidly proliferate and produce large amounts of specific antibody on re-exposure.
items.misconception: The pathogen becomes weaker after the primary exposure, so the body has an easier job.
items.random_wrong: The body produces a different, more powerful antibody during the secondary response.
items.why: Students attribute the difference to the pathogen rather than to memory cells (B and T memory lymphocytes), missing the role of immunological memory.

items.q: What is actually transferred to the body when you get a vaccine?
items.correct: Antigens (often from weakened, dead, or fragmented pathogens) that train the immune system to make memory cells.
items.misconception: Vaccines inject ready-made antibodies directly into the bloodstream.
items.random_wrong: Vaccines insert new genes into your DNA permanently.
items.why: Students confuse vaccines (which prompt your own immune response) with passive immunity (which delivers ready-made antibodies, e.g. antivenom).

items.q: Which type of immunity provides immediate but short-term protection through ready-made antibodies?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Passive artificial. “Active natural” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How do bacteria become resistant to an antibiotic?
items.correct: Random mutations conferring resistance are selected for
items.why: Correct answer: Random mutations conferring resistance are selected for. “They learn to resist” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If herd immunity threshold is 95% and a population is 10000, how many people must be immune?
items.why: Exact answer: 9500. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
